**Midterm 2 — Operation SILENT WATCH**

**Intrusion Detection Systems with Snort**

| Field | Value |
| ----- | ----- |
| Student 1 | Angel Arturo Urbano Lara \- 00470929 |
| Student 2 | Francisco Andres Cortes Cerna \- 00469838 |
| Date | May 3rd, 2026 |
| Course | Cybersecurity — Universidad Anáhuac Mayab |

**Part 1: Installation & Verification**  
**1.1 Network Topology:**  
**IP Addresses Discovered:**

| VM | Interface | IP Address | Role |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| snort-ids  | enp0s3  | 10.0.2.15  | Management (NAT)  |
| snort-ids  | enp0s8  | 10.0.1.10  | Attack segment |
| snort-ids  | enp0s8  | 10.0.2.10  | Target segment  |
| attacker  | eth1  | 10.0.1.30  | Attacker  |
| target  | eth1  | 10.0.2.20  | Target  |

**Network Topology Diagram:**  
attacker (10.0.1.30)  
        |  
        | 10.0.1.0/24  
        |  
snort-ids  
  enp0s8: 10.0.1.10  
  enp0s9: 10.0.2.10  
        |  
        | 10.0.2.0/24  
        |  
target (10.0.2.20)

**Connectivity test (attacker → target):**  
attacker:\~$ ping \-c 3 10.0.2.20  
PING 10.0.2.20 (10.0.2.20): 56 data bytes  
64 bytes from 10.0.2.20: seq=0 ttl=42 time=8.909 ms  
64 bytes from 10.0.2.20: seq=1 ttl=42 time=5.500 ms  
64 bytes from 10.0.2.20: seq=2 ttl=42 time=6.558 ms

\--- 10.0.2.20 ping statistics \---  
3 packets transmitted, 3 packets received, 0% packet loss  
round-trip min/avg/max \= 5.500/6.989/8.909 ms

**1.2 Snort Version & Test Run**  
**snort \--version output:**  
![][image1]

**snort \-T \-c /etc/snort/snort.conf output:**  
![][image2]  
![][image3]

**1.3 Monitoring Interface**  
**Selected interface**: enp0s8  
**Why this interface:** We selected enp0s8 because it is the interface on snort-ids connected to the attack segment (10.0.1.0/24), where the attacker VM is located. Traffic from the attacker to the protected target passes through this interface before being routed to the target network. We did not choose enp0s3 because it is the NAT/management interface used for Vagrant and Internet access, not for monitoring lab attack traffic.

**ip link show output:**  
![][image4]

**tcpdump sample confirming traffic visibility:**  
![][image5]

**Part 2: Snort Rule Syntax & Custom Rules**  
**Rule 1**  
**Complete rule:**  
alert tcp any any \-\> $HOME\_NET 21 (msg:"CUSTOM FTP anonymous login attempt"; flow:to\_server,established; content:"USER anonymous"; nocase; sid:1000001; rev:1;)

**What it detects:**  
This rule detects an FTP login attempt where the client sends the username anonymous to the target FTP service on port 21\.

**Why this event matters:**  
Anonymous FTP access can expose files or allow unauthorized users to interact with the server without a real account. An attacker using anonymous FTP may be performing reconnaissance or attempting to access publicly exposed files.

**Evidence the rule fires (from Part 4):**  
\[\*\*\] \[1:1000001:1\] CUSTOM FTP anonymous login attempt \[\*\*\]  
\[Priority: 0\]  
05/02-04:03:02.416362 10.0.1.30:44213 \-\> 10.0.2.20:21  
TCP TTL:64 TOS:0x0 ID:9389 IpLen:20 DgmLen:90 DF  
\*\*\*AP\*\*\* Seq: 0x38B8283C  Ack: 0x221FC49E  Win: 0x1F6  TcpLen: 32  
TCP Options (3) \=\> NOP NOP TS: 2713295569 1861239914

**Rule 2**  
**Complete rule:**  
alert tcp any any \-\> $HOME\_NET 80 (msg:"CUSTOM Nikto web scanner detected"; flow:to\_server,established; content:"Nikto"; nocase; sid:1000002; rev:1;)

**What it detects:**  
This rule detects HTTP requests to the protected web server that contain the string Nikto, which is commonly present in the User-Agent header of Nikto web scanner requests.

**Why this event matters:**  
Nikto is used to scan web servers for known vulnerabilities, dangerous files, and misconfigurations. Detecting this traffic indicates that someone is actively probing the web application and may be preparing further exploitation.

**Evidence the rule fires (from Part 4):**  
\[\*\*\] \[1:1000002:1\] CUSTOM Nikto web scanner detected \[\*\*\]  
\[Priority: 0\]  
05/02-03:39:49.826239 10.0.1.30:48496 \-\> 10.0.2.20:80  
TCP TTL:64 TOS:0x0 ID:61787 IpLen:20 DgmLen:192 DF  
\*\*\*AP\*\*\* Seq: 0x389D9FC4  Ack: 0xE418318  Win: 0x12BA  TcpLen: 32  
TCP Options (3) \=\> NOP NOP TS: 2711902549 1859846642

\[\*\*\] \[1:1520:6\] WEB-MISC server-info access \[\*\*\]  
\[Classification: access to a potentially vulnerable web application\] \[Priority: 2\]  
05/02-03:39:49.826239 10.0.1.30:48496 \-\> 10.0.2.20:80  
TCP TTL:63 TOS:0x0 ID:53449 IpLen:20 DgmLen:192 DF  
\*\*\*AP\*\*\* Seq: 0x389D9FC4  Ack: 0xE418318  Win: 0x8A00  TcpLen: 32

\[\*\*\] \[1:1560:6\] WEB-MISC /doc/ access \[\*\*\]  
\[Classification: access to a potentially vulnerable web application\] \[Priority: 2\]  
05/02-03:39:49.843282 10.0.1.30:48496 \-\> 10.0.2.20:80  
TCP TTL:63 TOS:0x0 ID:53451 IpLen:20 DgmLen:192 DF  
\*\*\*AP\*\*\* Seq: 0x389DA0E1  Ack: 0xE418564  Win: 0x8A00  TcpLen: 32

**Part 3: Configuration & Community Rules**  
**3.1 snort.conf Configuration**  
**Relevant snort.conf excerpt:**  
![][image6]

**3.2 Community Rules Download**  
**Commands run:**  
cd /tmp  
wget https://www.snort.org/downloads/community/community-rules.tar.gz  
tar \-xzf community-rules.tar.gz  
ls community-rules/  
sudo cp community-rules/community.rules /etc/snort/rules/  
echo 'include $RULE\_PATH/community.rules' | sudo tee \-a /etc/snort/snort.conf

**ls /etc/snort/rules/ output after installation:**  
![][image7]

**3.3 Full Configuration Validation**  
**snort \-T \-c /etc/snort/snort.conf output (final, with all rules loaded):**  
4620 Snort rules read  
    3929 detection rules  
Snort successfully validated the configuration\!

**Part 4: Detection Demonstration**  
**4.1 Attack 1 — nmap Reconnaissance**  
**Commands run (from attacker VM):**  
sudo nmap \-sS 10.0.2.20  
sudo nmap \-sV 10.0.2.20  
sudo nmap \-O 10.0.2.20  
sudo nmap \-sN 10.0.2.20

**Snort alert output:**  
\[\*\*\] \[1:469:3\] ICMP PING NMAP \[\*\*\]  
\[Classification: Attempted Information Leak\] \[Priority: 2\]  
05/02-03:10:51.193543 10.0.1.30 \-\> 10.0.2.20  
ICMP TTL:44 TOS:0x0 ID:33323 IpLen:20 DgmLen:28  
Type:8  Code:0  ID:1206   Seq:0  ECHO  
\[Xref \=\> http://www.whitehats.com/info/IDS162\]

\[\*\*\] \[1:1418:11\] SNMP request tcp \[\*\*\]  
\[Classification: Attempted Information Leak\] \[Priority: 2\]  
05/02-03:10:52.344817 10.0.1.30:53216 \-\> 10.0.2.20:161  
TCP TTL:49 TOS:0x0 ID:59189 IpLen:20 DgmLen:44  
\*\*\*\*\*\*S\* Seq: 0xC7547842  Ack: 0x0  Win: 0x400  TcpLen: 24  
TCP Options (1) \=\> MSS: 1460

\[\*\*\] \[1:1421:11\] SNMP AgentX/tcp request \[\*\*\]  
\[Classification: Attempted Information Leak\] \[Priority: 2\]  
05/02-03:10:52.644568 10.0.1.30:53216 \-\> 10.0.2.20:705  
TCP TTL:58 TOS:0x0 ID:23983 IpLen:20 DgmLen:44  
\*\*\*\*\*\*S\* Seq: 0xC7547842  Ack: 0x0  Win: 0x400  TcpLen: 24  
TCP Options (1) \=\> MSS: 1460

\[\*\*\] \[1:1228:7\] SCAN nmap XMAS \[\*\*\]  
\[Classification: Attempted Information Leak\] \[Priority: 2\]  
05/02-03:10:56.157261 10.0.1.30:52026 \-\> 10.0.2.20:1  
TCP TTL:50 TOS:0x0 ID:50347 IpLen:20 DgmLen:60  
\*\*U\*P\*\*F Seq: 0x7D49F1F3  Ack: 0xA268346B  Win: 0xFFFF  TcpLen: 40  UrgPtr: 0x0  
TCP Options (5) \=\> WS: 15 NOP MSS: 265 TS: 4294967295 0 SackOK

**Analysis — which scans were detected and which were not:**  
The nmap reconnaissance activity was detected by Snort. The alert log showed multiple signatures related to scanning, including “ICMP PING NMAP”, “SNMP request tcp”, “SNMP AgentX/tcp request”, and “SCAN nmap XMAS”. This confirms that Snort observed traffic from the attacker host 10.0.1.30 to the target host 10.0.2.20. The SYN and service/version scans generated TCP probing behavior that triggered SNMP-related reconnaissance alerts, while the OS/fingerprinting or unusual flag scans triggered the “nmap XMAS” signature. Not every command produced a uniquely named alert, but the overall reconnaissance activity was detected.

**4.2 Attack 2 — hping3 Flood**  
**Commands run (from attacker VM):**  
sudo hping3 \-S \-p 80 \--flood \-c 100 10.0.2.20  
sudo hping3 \--icmp \--flood \-c 100 10.0.2.20

**Snort alert output:**  
\[\*\*\] \[1:469:3\] ICMP PING NMAP \[\*\*\]  
\[Classification: Attempted Information Leak\] \[Priority: 2\]  
05/02-03:16:50.872389 10.0.1.30 \-\> 10.0.2.20  
ICMP TTL:64 TOS:0x0 ID:55832 IpLen:20 DgmLen:28  
Type:8  Code:0  ID:59913   Seq:35132  ECHO  
\[Xref \=\> http://www.whitehats.com/info/IDS162\]

\[\*\*\] \[1:469:3\] ICMP PING NMAP \[\*\*\]  
\[Classification: Attempted Information Leak\] \[Priority: 2\]  
05/02-03:16:50.873249 10.0.1.30 \-\> 10.0.2.20  
ICMP TTL:64 TOS:0x0 ID:42752 IpLen:20 DgmLen:28  
Type:8  Code:0  ID:59913   Seq:35644  ECHO  
\[Xref \=\> http://www.whitehats.com/info/IDS162\]

**Analysis:**  
The hping3 test generated two different types of traffic. The first command sent TCP SYN packets to port 80, while the second command sent ICMP echo request packets. Snort clearly detected the ICMP flood and generated repeated “ICMP PING NMAP” alerts from 10.0.1.30 to 10.0.2.20. The SYN flood traffic was confirmed with tcpdump on the IDS interface, but the Snort alert log did not show a clear SYN-flood-specific alert. This suggests that the enabled rule set detected the ICMP burst but did not include, or did not trigger, a threshold-based SYN flood signature for the limited 100-packet test.

**4.3 Attack 3 — Nikto Web Scanner**  
**Command run:**  
/usr/bin/nikto.pl \-h 10.0.2.20 \-p 80

**Nikto output summary:**  
\- Nikto v2.1.6  
\+ Target IP:          10.0.2.20  
\+ Target Hostname:    10.0.2.20  
\+ Target Port:        80  
\+ Server: nginx  
\+ Server leaks inodes via ETags, header found with file /  
\+ The anti-clickjacking X-Frame-Options header is not present.  
\+ The X-XSS-Protection header is not defined.  
\+ The X-Content-Type-Options header is not set.  
\+ No CGI Directories found.  
\+ 7535 requests: 0 error(s) and 4 item(s) reported on remote host  
\+ 1 host(s) tested

**Snort alert output:**  
\[\*\*\] \[1:1000002:1\] CUSTOM Nikto web scanner detected \[\*\*\]  
\[Priority: 0\]  
05/02-03:39:49.826239 10.0.1.30:48496 \-\> 10.0.2.20:80  
TCP TTL:64 TOS:0x0 ID:61787 IpLen:20 DgmLen:192 DF  
\*\*\*AP\*\*\* Seq: 0x389D9FC4  Ack: 0xE418318  Win: 0x12BA  TcpLen: 32  
TCP Options (3) \=\> NOP NOP TS: 2711902549 1859846642

\[\*\*\] \[1:1520:6\] WEB-MISC server-info access \[\*\*\]  
\[Classification: access to a potentially vulnerable web application\] \[Priority: 2\]  
05/02-03:39:49.826239 10.0.1.30:48496 \-\> 10.0.2.20:80  
TCP TTL:63 TOS:0x0 ID:53449 IpLen:20 DgmLen:192 DF  
\*\*\*AP\*\*\* Seq: 0x389D9FC4  Ack: 0xE418318  Win: 0x8A00  TcpLen: 32

\[\*\*\] \[1:1560:6\] WEB-MISC /doc/ access \[\*\*\]  
\[Classification: access to a potentially vulnerable web application\] \[Priority: 2\]  
05/02-03:39:49.843282 10.0.1.30:48496 \-\> 10.0.2.20:80  
TCP TTL:63 TOS:0x0 ID:53451 IpLen:20 DgmLen:192 DF  
\*\*\*AP\*\*\* Seq: 0x389DA0E1  Ack: 0xE418564  Win: 0x8A00  TcpLen: 32

**Did custom Rule 2 fire? Evidence:**  
Yes. Custom Rule 2 fired multiple times. Evidence:  
\[\*\*\] \[1:1000002:1\] CUSTOM Nikto web scanner detected \[\*\*\]  
\[Priority: 0\]  
05/02-03:39:49.826239 10.0.1.30:48496 \-\> 10.0.2.20:80  
TCP TTL:64 TOS:0x0 ID:61787 IpLen:20 DgmLen:192 DF  
\*\*\*AP\*\*\* Seq: 0x389D9FC4  Ack: 0xE418318  Win: 0x12BA  TcpLen: 32

**4.4 FTP Interaction**  
**FTP terminal session (from attacker VM):**  
![][image8]

**Snort alert output:**  
\[\*\*\] \[1:1000001:1\] CUSTOM FTP anonymous login attempt \[\*\*\]  
\[Priority: 0\]  
05/02-04:03:02.416362 10.0.1.30:44213 \-\> 10.0.2.20:21  
TCP TTL:64 TOS:0x0 ID:9389 IpLen:20 DgmLen:90 DF  
\*\*\*AP\*\*\* Seq: 0x38B8283C  Ack: 0x221FC49E  Win: 0x1F6  TcpLen: 32  
TCP Options (3) \=\> NOP NOP TS: 2713295569 1861239914

**Did custom Rule 1 fire? Evidence:**  
Yes. Custom Rule 1 fired when Snort observed the manually generated FTP command “USER anonymous” sent from the attacker host 10.0.1.30 to the target FTP service at 10.0.2.20:21.

TCP Options (3) \=\> NOP NOP TS: 2711902584 1859846676

\[Classification: access to a potentially vulnerable web application\] \[Priority: 2\]

**Part 5: Analysis & Reflection**  
**Detection Coverage Table**

| Attack | Detected? | Rule Source | Alert Message |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| nmap SYN scan | Yes | Community | ICMP PING NMAP / SNMP request tcp |
| nmap OS scan | Yes | Community | SCAN nmap XMAS / ICMP PING NMAP |
| nmap NULL scan | Partially | Community | SCAN nmap XMAS |
| hping3 SYN flood | Partially | None / tcpdump confirmed traffic | No clear SYN-flood-specific Snort alert |
| hping3 ICMP flood | Yes | Community | ICMP PING NMAP |
| Nikto web scan | Yes | Custom \+ Community | CUSTOM Nikto web scanner detected / WEB-MISC server-status access |
| FTP anonymous login | Yes | Custom \+ Community | CUSTOM FTP anonymous login attempt / POLICY FTP anonymous login attempt |

**Blind Spots**

Some attacks were detected clearly, while others were only partially detected. The most important blind spot was the hping3 SYN flood. The TCP SYN packets did reach the IDS interface, and tcpdump confirmed traffic from `10.0.1.30` to `10.0.2.20:80`, but Snort did not generate a clear SYN-flood-specific alert in the log. This probably happened because the enabled rules did not include a threshold-based SYN flood rule, or because the test was limited to 100 packets and did not pass the detection threshold. A better rule for this case would count many SYN packets from the same source to the same destination within a short time window. The nmap NULL scan was also not labeled exactly as “NULL scan”; instead, Snort classified some unusual scan traffic as “SCAN nmap XMAS.” This still shows detection, but not perfect classification. Overall, Snort was effective at detecting known signatures, but it depends heavily on rule coverage, thresholds, and correct configuration.

**IDS vs. IPS**

Based on this lab, an IDS and an IPS have different operational roles. An IDS, like the Snort setup used here, monitors traffic and generates alerts when suspicious activity is detected. It helps defenders see what is happening in the network, but it does not automatically stop the traffic. An IPS, on the other hand, is placed inline and can block, drop, or reject malicious traffic in real time. I would deploy an IDS when visibility and investigation are the main goals, especially in environments where false positives could interrupt important services. I would deploy an IPS in higher-risk areas where blocking attacks quickly is more important, such as at a network perimeter or in front of critical services. However, running an IPS in production has risks. If the rules are too aggressive or poorly tuned, the IPS could block legitimate users, break applications, or cause outages. For that reason, IDS monitoring is useful first, and IPS blocking should only be enabled after testing and tuning the rules.

**Blue Team Memo to CorpIntranet**

TO: CorpIntranet Security Team  
 FROM: Angel Urbano and Francisco Cortes  
 RE: Security Posture Recommendations

* Improve IDS rule coverage by adding custom rules for organization-specific risks, such as anonymous FTP attempts, suspicious web scanner traffic, and repeated SYN packets.  
* Tune detection thresholds to better identify flood behavior without generating too many false positives during normal traffic spikes.  
* Continuously monitor alert logs and review them in real time during suspicious activity, because important events can be missed if logs are only reviewed after the fact.  
* Reduce exposed services where possible, especially FTP, and disable anonymous access unless there is a strong business reason.  
* Validate network topology and IDS interface placement regularly, because Snort can only detect attacks if the traffic actually passes through the monitored interface.
